answer.oneLiner: A RESTful API is stateless, identifies resources with URIs, uses HTTP verbs to express actions, and uses HTTP status codes to signal outcomes. Each request carries everything the server needs - no session on the server.
answer.detail: REST stands for Representational State Transfer. It is an architectural style, not a protocol. The six constraints Roy Fielding defined are: client-server separation, statelessness, cacheability, uniform interface, layered system, and optional code-on-demand. In practice, most teams focus on three things: statelessness (each request is self-contained, which means any server instance can handle any request - this is how you scale horizontally), resource-based URIs using nouns not verbs (/orders/42 not /getOrder?id=42), and correct use of HTTP verbs with matching status codes. For example, GET reads, POST creates, PUT replaces, PATCH partially updates, DELETE removes. Simplified: think of REST as a contract where the URL says WHAT you are working with and the HTTP method says WHAT you want to do to it.
keyPoints: Stateless - every request is self-contained, no server-side session, which enables horizontal scaling
URIs are nouns that identify resources - /orders/42, not /getOrder or /fetchOrderById
HTTP verbs carry the action: GET read, POST create, PUT/PATCH update, DELETE remove
Status codes signal the outcome: 2xx success, 4xx client error, 5xx server error - never 200 for an error

code.note: The good version uses a noun-based hierarchical URI, the correct HTTP verb, and returns the right status code for each outcome.

answer.analogy: The URL is the address; the HTTP verb is what you do there.
redFlags: Saying REST is a protocol - it is an architectural style, not a protocol like HTTP or SOAP
Putting verbs in URLs like /getOrder or /createUser instead of using HTTP methods
Returning HTTP 200 with an error message in the body instead of a proper 4xx or 5xx status
Confusing stateless with sessionless - stateless means each request is self-contained, not that users cannot log in

followUpsQA.q: Why does statelessness help with scaling?
followUpsQA.a: Because no server instance holds session state, any load balancer can route any request to any instance - you scale by adding servers, not by managing sticky sessions.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between safe and idempotent methods?
followUpsQA.a: Safe methods (GET, HEAD) have no side effects at all. Idempotent methods (GET, PUT, DELETE) can have side effects but calling them multiple times gives the same result as calling once.

followUpsQA.q: When should you use POST versus PUT to create a resource?
followUpsQA.a: Use POST when the server assigns the ID - POST /orders creates and returns the new ID. Use PUT when the client knows the ID upfront - PUT /orders/abc123 sets that exact resource.

followUpsQA.q: What HTTP status code should a successful resource creation return?
followUpsQA.a: 201 Created, with a Location header pointing to the new resource URL - not 200 OK.

followUpsQA.q: Is REST the same as JSON over HTTP?
followUpsQA.a: No - REST is an architectural style that can use any representation format. JSON is the most common, but REST can return XML, HTML, or binary data.

dialogue.text: What makes an API RESTful?

A RESTful API follows the REST constraints: stateless communication, resource-based URIs using nouns, HTTP verbs for actions, and proper status codes for outcomes. The most important one in practice is statelessness - each request is self-contained with no server-side session.
You mentioned statelessness helps with scaling. Can you explain that connection?

Because no server instance owns session state, any load balancer can route any request to any instance. You scale horizontally by adding more servers without worrying about sticky sessions or a shared session store. In an e-commerce platform handling Black Friday traffic, that matters a lot.
Give me an example of a non-RESTful URL and how you would fix it.

POST /api/getCustomerOrders is broken twice - it uses a verb in the URL and the wrong HTTP method. The REST version is GET /api/customers/42/orders. The URL identifies the resource (customer 42's orders), and the HTTP verb says what to do with it.
What status codes matter most and where do developers get them wrong?

The most common mistake is returning 200 with an error body. If a customer is not found, return 404 Not Found. If validation fails, return 400 Bad Request. A successful create should be 201 Created with a Location header, not 200 OK.
Is REST a protocol?

No - REST is an architectural style defined by Roy Fielding in his 2000 dissertation. HTTP is the protocol. You can technically implement REST over other protocols, but HTTP is by far the most common. The six REST constraints are independent of the transport.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the difference between PUT and PATCH?

answer.oneLiner: PUT replaces the entire resource with whatever you send. PATCH applies a partial update to only the fields you include. If you PUT a customer record without the phone number, the phone number is erased.
answer.detail: PUT is a full replacement operation. The RFC says the server should store the request body as the new state of the resource. This means any field you omit gets reset to null or its default. PUT is idempotent by definition - sending the same body 10 times leaves the resource in the same final state as sending it once. PATCH is a partial update. You only send the fields you want to change. PATCH is not inherently idempotent - a 'set status to shipped' PATCH is idempotent, but an 'increment retry count' PATCH is not. In ASP.NET Core, PATCH commonly uses the JsonPatch standard (RFC 6902) where you send an array of operation objects like add, remove, replace, move. Simplified: PUT says 'the resource is now exactly this'; PATCH says 'change just these fields'.
keyPoints: PUT sends the full resource representation - missing fields get reset to null or default
PATCH sends only the changed fields - unmentioned fields stay untouched
PUT is always idempotent; PATCH is idempotent only if the operation is a set, not an increment
JsonPatch (RFC 6902) is the formal standard for PATCH bodies in ASP.NET Core using Microsoft.AspNetCore.JsonPatch

code.note: JsonPatchDocument applies only the specified operations to the existing record, leaving all other fields intact.

answer.analogy: PUT is repainting the whole wall; PATCH is touching up one corner.
redFlags: Saying PUT and PATCH are interchangeable - they have different semantics and idempotency guarantees
Not knowing that a PUT with missing fields resets those fields to null
Saying PATCH is always idempotent - it depends on the operation type
Confusing PATCH with a partial POST - PATCH operates on an existing resource identified by its URI

followUpsQA.q: What happens to a field omitted from a PUT request?
followUpsQA.a: It gets reset to null or its default value because PUT replaces the entire resource - the server treats the request body as the new complete state.

followUpsQA.q: Is PATCH idempotent?
followUpsQA.a: Not inherently. A PATCH that sets a field to a fixed value is idempotent. A PATCH that increments a counter is not, because each call changes the result.

followUpsQA.q: What is JsonPatch and what package provides it in ASP.NET Core?
followUpsQA.a: JsonPatch (RFC 6902) is a format for describing changes as an array of operations like replace and remove. The Microsoft.AspNetCore.JsonPatch package provides the JsonPatchDocument<T> type.

followUpsQA.q: When should you choose PUT over PATCH?
followUpsQA.a: When the client always sends the complete resource and you want the simplicity of a full replacement - for example, updating a user's entire profile settings page where all fields are always present.

dialogue.text: What is the difference between PUT and PATCH?

PUT replaces the entire resource with the body you send. If you omit a field, that field gets wiped. PATCH is a partial update - you send only the fields you want to change, and everything else stays untouched.
You said PUT wipes fields. Walk me through a real scenario where that causes a bug.

An e-commerce order has customer name, shipping address, and payment reference. If a mobile app only sends the updated shipping address via PUT and omits the payment reference, the payment reference gets nulled out in the database. That is a silent data loss bug, hard to spot until a payment fails.
Is PATCH idempotent?

Not always. A PATCH that sets a status field to 'shipped' is idempotent - repeating it leaves the same state. A PATCH that says 'increment retry count by 1' is not idempotent because each repeat changes the value.
How do you implement PATCH properly in ASP.NET Core?

I use JsonPatchDocument<T> from the Microsoft.AspNetCore.JsonPatch package. The client sends an array of operations in RFC 6902 format, like replace /shippingAddress/city. I call patch.ApplyTo(entity, ModelState) and then validate ModelState before saving.
What is idempotency and why does it matter for retries?

An idempotent operation produces the same end state no matter how many times you call it. It matters because networks are unreliable - if a client retries a timed-out PUT or DELETE, there is no harm. But retrying a non-idempotent POST can create duplicate orders, which is why payment systems use idempotency keys.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you handle exceptions globally in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: You register a global error handler using UseExceptionHandler middleware or the IExceptionHandler interface in .NET 8. It catches all unhandled exceptions in one place and returns a consistent ProblemDetails response instead of leaking stack traces.
answer.detail: There are three main approaches. First, UseExceptionHandler with a lambda - you configure an inline pipeline that reads the exception from IExceptionHandlerFeature and writes a structured error response. Second, a custom exception handling middleware - a class in the pipeline that wraps the next() call in a try/catch and maps exception types to status codes. Third, in .NET 8 you can implement the IExceptionHandler interface and register it with AddExceptionHandler, which is the cleanest approach because you can register multiple handlers tried in order. All approaches should return RFC 7807 ProblemDetails format so clients always get the same error shape. You map known domain exceptions - NotFoundException to 404, ValidationException to 400, everything else to 500. In production you never expose stack traces; you log them server-side and return a generic message to the client.
keyPoints: Centralizes error handling so you never scatter try/catch across every controller method
Return RFC 7807 ProblemDetails format for consistent, machine-readable error responses
Map domain exceptions to HTTP status codes - NotFoundException to 404, ValidationException to 400
Exception handler middleware must be registered first in the pipeline so it wraps everything downstream
Never expose stack traces to clients in production - log details server-side only

code.note: IExceptionHandler centralizes error mapping and returns ProblemDetails without touching individual controllers.

answer.analogy: One bouncer at the door catches every problem - not one per room.
redFlags: Saying try/catch in each controller is acceptable for production - it scatters logic and is easy to miss
Not knowing RFC 7807 ProblemDetails - interviewers expect you to name the standard error format
Returning stack traces or internal exception messages to API clients in production
Confusing UseExceptionHandler with UseStatusCodePages - the former catches thrown exceptions, the latter handles specific status codes like 404 from routing

followUpsQA.q: What is ProblemDetails and why use it?
followUpsQA.a: ProblemDetails is the RFC 7807 standard for HTTP error responses. It gives clients a consistent JSON shape with type, title, status, detail, and instance fields so they can parse errors programmatically.

followUpsQA.q: What is the .NET 8 way to handle exceptions globally?
followUpsQA.a: Implement the IExceptionHandler interface, register it with builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<T>(), and add app.UseExceptionHandler() to the pipeline. You can chain multiple handlers tried in order.

followUpsQA.q: Where must the exception handler middleware be placed in the pipeline?
followUpsQA.a: At the very top, before all other middleware, so it wraps everything and can catch exceptions thrown anywhere downstream.

followUpsQA.q: How do you turn a domain NotFoundException into a 404?
followUpsQA.a: In the exception handler you check the exception type with is or a switch expression, set ctx.Response.StatusCode = 404, and write a ProblemDetails body with that status.

dialogue.text: How do you handle exceptions globally in ASP.NET Core?

I use a global mechanism rather than try/catch in each controller. In .NET 8 I prefer implementing IExceptionHandler - you get a clean class that maps exception types to status codes and returns a ProblemDetails response. Register it with AddExceptionHandler and add UseExceptionHandler to the pipeline.
Why not just try/catch in each controller action?

It scatters error handling everywhere, is easy to forget on new actions, and leads to inconsistent response shapes. A global handler means one place to format errors, one place to log them, and a consistent ProblemDetails body that API clients can always rely on.
How do you map a domain exception like OrderNotFoundException to a 404?

Inside TryHandleAsync I check the exception type - if ex is OrderNotFoundException I set the status to 404, otherwise 500. Then I write a ProblemDetails JSON body with that status and a user-friendly title. The full exception details go to the logger, never to the client.
Where must UseExceptionHandler be placed and why?

It must be registered first in the middleware pipeline, before everything else. That way it wraps all downstream middleware in its error net. If you place it after authentication, exceptions from authentication escape it.
What is ProblemDetails?

It is the RFC 7807 standard for HTTP error responses. The JSON shape has type, title, status, detail, and instance fields. Using it means every client gets the same error format they can parse reliably, instead of guessing the error shape per endpoint.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the difference between Authentication and Authorization?

answer.oneLiner: Authentication answers WHO are you - it verifies identity. Authorization answers WHAT are you allowed to do - it enforces access rules. Authentication always runs first because you cannot check permissions for an unknown user.
answer.detail: Authentication validates credentials and builds the ClaimsPrincipal - the identity object that represents the logged-in user with all their claims. Common mechanisms are JWT bearer tokens, cookies, and API keys. In ASP.NET Core, UseAuthentication() middleware reads the incoming token or cookie, validates it, and populates HttpContext.User with the ClaimsPrincipal. Authorization then uses that ClaimsPrincipal to decide access. It can check a role claim ([Authorize(Roles='Admin')]), a policy (a set of requirements evaluated by an AuthorizationHandler), or simple claim presence. UseAuthorization() runs after UseAuthentication() in the pipeline. If authentication fails (no token or invalid token), the response is 401 Unauthorized. If authentication succeeds but the user lacks the required permission, the response is 403 Forbidden. These two status codes are distinct - confusing them is a common interview red flag.
keyPoints: Authentication = WHO are you - validates credentials and builds ClaimsPrincipal on HttpContext.User
Authorization = WHAT can you do - checks roles, policies, or claims against the ClaimsPrincipal
Pipeline order is non-negotiable: UseAuthentication() must come before UseAuthorization()
401 Unauthorized means not authenticated; 403 Forbidden means authenticated but not permitted

code.note: Swapping the order means authorization always sees an empty identity and rejects every request.

answer.analogy: AuthN is showing your passport at the border; AuthZ is whether your visa lets you through.
redFlags: Saying 401 and 403 are the same thing - 401 means unknown identity, 403 means known but blocked
Not knowing that UseAuthentication must come before UseAuthorization in the pipeline
Confusing authentication mechanism (JWT, cookie) with authorization strategy (role, policy)
Saying authentication creates permissions - it creates the identity; authorization grants or denies access

followUpsQA.q: What does UseAuthentication produce for UseAuthorization to consume?
followUpsQA.a: A ClaimsPrincipal populated on HttpContext.User. It contains the user's identity and all their claims such as role, email, and custom business claims.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between 401 and 403?
followUpsQA.a: 401 Unauthorized means the request has no valid identity - the user needs to log in. 403 Forbidden means the identity is valid but lacks the required permission.

followUpsQA.q: How does policy-based authorization differ from role-based?
followUpsQA.a: Role-based checks a single role claim. Policy-based can evaluate multiple claims, custom handlers, and complex business rules - for example, a policy that checks a user both has the Admin role and has their email confirmed.

followUpsQA.q: Where does JWT validation happen - in authentication or authorization?
followUpsQA.a: In authentication. The JWT bearer middleware validates the signature and expiry and builds the ClaimsPrincipal. Authorization just reads the resulting claims.

dialogue.text: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?

Authentication answers WHO are you - it validates credentials and builds the ClaimsPrincipal. Authorization answers WHAT can you do - it evaluates that identity against roles, policies, or claims. Authentication always runs first.
What does authentication actually produce that authorization then uses?

A ClaimsPrincipal on HttpContext.User. For a JWT bearer request, UseAuthentication validates the token signature and expiry, then deserializes the claims - user ID, roles, email - into that principal. UseAuthorization then checks that principal against the [Authorize] attribute.
If I accidentally register UseAuthorization before UseAuthentication, what happens?

Authorization runs against an empty ClaimsPrincipal because the token has not been validated yet. Every [Authorize] endpoint rejects the request with 401 even when the caller sends a perfectly valid JWT.
A logged-in user hits an admin endpoint they are not allowed to access. What status code do you return and why?

403 Forbidden. The user is authenticated - we know who they are - but they lack the required permission. 401 would be wrong here because 401 means we do not know who the caller is. The distinction matters because clients handle them differently.
How do you implement a policy that requires both a role and a custom claim?

I define an IAuthorizationRequirement, write an AuthorizationHandler<T> that checks both conditions using context.User.IsInRole() and context.User.HasClaim(), and register it in AddAuthorization with a named policy. Then I use [Authorize(Policy='SeniorManager')] on the endpoint.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

answer.oneLiner: The client sends credentials to a login endpoint and gets back a signed JWT. On every subsequent request the client sends that JWT in the Authorization header. The server validates the signature and expiry locally - no database lookup, no session.
answer.detail: A JWT has three Base64-encoded parts separated by dots: Header.Payload.Signature. The header names the algorithm (HS256, RS256). The payload holds claims - sub (user ID), role, exp (expiry). The signature is computed by the server using a secret key (HMAC) or private key (RSA). On validation, the server recomputes the signature from the header and payload and compares it to the received signature. If they match and the exp claim is in the future, the token is valid. Because the server can validate the signature locally, JWT is stateless - no session store needed. This means any server instance in a cluster can validate any token. The payload is Base64-encoded, not encrypted - anyone with the token can decode and read the claims. Never put secrets, passwords, or confidential PII in the payload. The standard pattern is a short-lived access token (15 minutes) paired with a longer-lived refresh token. When the access token expires, the client uses the refresh token to get a new access token without the user re-entering credentials. For instant revocation you need a server-side denylist or introspection endpoint because JWTs are self-contained.
keyPoints: JWT structure is Header.Payload.Signature - the payload is signed not encrypted, so it is readable by anyone
Stateless validation - the server checks the signature and exp claim locally without any database or session store
Short-lived access tokens (15 minutes) plus refresh tokens minimize damage from a stolen token
Never store sensitive data in the JWT payload - it is only protected by the signature, not encryption

code.note: The good version uses a 15-minute expiry, includes only safe claims, and signs with a strong HMAC key stored in config.

answer.analogy: A JWT is a tamper-evident wristband - readable by all but forgeable by none.
redFlags: Saying the JWT payload is encrypted - it is Base64-encoded and readable by anyone with the token
Not knowing the three parts of a JWT: Header, Payload, Signature
Suggesting long-lived access tokens like 24 hours or more - this is a security risk
Confusing authentication (validating the JWT) with authorization (checking the claims inside it)

followUpsQA.q: What are the three parts of a JWT?
followUpsQA.a: Header (algorithm and token type), Payload (claims like user ID, roles, expiry), and Signature (the cryptographic proof of integrity). All three are Base64URL-encoded and separated by dots.

followUpsQA.q: Why use short-lived access tokens?
followUpsQA.a: To limit the damage if a token is stolen. A stolen token that expires in 15 minutes is far less dangerous than one valid for 24 hours. Pair it with a refresh token so the user experience is not disrupted.

followUpsQA.q: How do you invalidate a JWT before it expires?
followUpsQA.a: JWTs are self-contained so you cannot invalidate them without server state. Common approaches are a token denylist stored in Redis that you check on each request, or using short expiry times to minimize the window.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between HS256 and RS256 signing?
followUpsQA.a: HS256 uses a shared HMAC secret - both issuer and validator need the same key. RS256 uses an asymmetric RSA key pair - the issuer signs with the private key and validators check with the public key, which is safer for distributed systems.

followUpsQA.q: Where should the client store the JWT?
followUpsQA.a: HttpOnly cookies protect against XSS because JavaScript cannot read them. localStorage is convenient but vulnerable to XSS attacks. For banking or healthcare apps, HttpOnly cookies with SameSite=Strict are the safer choice.

dialogue.text: Walk me through the JWT authentication flow.

The client POST /auth/login with credentials. The server validates them and returns a signed JWT containing claims like user ID and role. On every subsequent request the client sends it as Authorization: Bearer. The server validates the signature and the exp claim locally - no session, no database lookup.
Is the payload encrypted? What data is safe to put in it?

No - the payload is Base64-encoded, not encrypted. Anyone holding the token can decode and read it. Safe claims are user ID, role, and email. Never put credit card numbers, passwords, or confidential PII in there. The signature only proves the payload was not tampered with.
A customer's JWT is stolen. What limits the damage?

Short expiry on the access token - typically 15 minutes. Pair it with a refresh token so the user does not have to re-login constantly. For immediate revocation you need a server-side check like a Redis denylist, because pure JWTs are self-contained and valid until they expire.
How does the server verify the signature?

It recomputes the HMAC or RSA signature from the received header and payload using its key. If the result matches the signature in the token and the exp is in the future, the token is valid. This is all local computation - no external call needed, which is why JWT scales well.
What is the difference between HS256 and RS256?

HS256 is HMAC with a shared secret - every service that validates tokens needs to know that secret, which creates a distribution problem. RS256 uses an RSA key pair - the auth server signs with the private key and publishes the public key at a JWKS endpoint. Other services download the public key to validate tokens without ever knowing the private key.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is API versioning, why is it needed, and what approaches exist?

answer.oneLiner: API versioning lets you change an API without breaking existing clients. You give old clients a stable contract on v1 while new clients move to v2. Without versioning, any breaking change breaks every consumer immediately.
answer.detail: A breaking change is anything that forces a client to update - removing a field, renaming a property, changing a data type, or changing the behavior of an existing endpoint. Versioning gives you a controlled way to introduce these changes. The four common approaches are: URL path versioning (/api/v1/orders - most common and most discoverable), query string versioning (?api-version=1.0), header versioning (api-version: 1.0 in a custom header), and media type versioning (Accept: application/vnd.myapi.v1+json). URL path is the most visible and cache-friendly because the version is part of the URL, making it easy to test and document. Header and media-type keep URLs clean and are more REST-pure (the URL identifies the resource, not the version), but they are harder to test in a browser. In ASP.NET Core the Asp.Versioning NuGet package handles all four strategies. You annotate controllers with [ApiVersion] and configure a default version for clients that do not specify one. Deprecation is important - mark old versions as deprecated so clients get warned, but keep them running until you have confirmed all consumers have migrated.
keyPoints: Versioning isolates breaking changes so old clients keep working on v1 while new ones use v2
URL path (/api/v1/orders) is the most common approach - explicit, cache-friendly, easy to test
The Asp.Versioning NuGet package handles all versioning strategies in ASP.NET Core
Mark deprecated versions with [ApiVersion("1.0", Deprecated = true)] so clients are warned before removal

code.note: Both versions coexist in the same controller. V1 is marked deprecated so clients receive a warning header.

answer.analogy: Versioning is a promise to old clients: your lane stays open while we build a new one.
redFlags: Saying you would just update the existing endpoint - that breaks every client immediately with no migration path
Not knowing the Asp.Versioning package exists for ASP.NET Core
Confusing breaking changes with non-breaking additions - adding a new optional field is not a breaking change
Not mentioning deprecation - you need a plan to sunset old versions, not just add new ones

followUpsQA.q: What counts as a breaking change in an API?
followUpsQA.a: Removing or renaming a field, changing a field's data type, changing required status, removing an endpoint, or changing the meaning of an existing status code. Adding optional fields or new endpoints is non-breaking.

followUpsQA.q: What NuGet package handles API versioning in ASP.NET Core?
followUpsQA.a: Asp.Versioning (formerly Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.Versioning). It supports URL path, query string, header, and media-type strategies.

followUpsQA.q: What is the trade-off between URL versioning and header versioning?
followUpsQA.a: URL versioning is visible, cacheable, and easy to test in a browser. Header versioning keeps URLs clean and is more REST-pure, but it requires tooling support to discover and test.

followUpsQA.q: How do you deprecate a version without removing it immediately?
followUpsQA.a: Use [ApiVersion('1.0', Deprecated = true)]. The framework adds a Sunset or api-deprecated-versions header to responses, giving clients time to migrate before you remove the version.

dialogue.text: What is API versioning and why is it needed?

API versioning lets you evolve an API without breaking existing clients. When you need a breaking change - removing a field, changing a type - you create v2 and old clients stay on v1. Without versioning, any breaking change immediately breaks every consumer.
What counts as a breaking change?

Removing or renaming a field, changing a data type, removing an endpoint, or changing the semantics of an existing status code. Adding a new optional field or a new endpoint is non-breaking - clients that do not know about it just ignore it.
Walk me through the four versioning strategies.

URL path like /api/v1/orders is most common - explicit and cache-friendly. Query string like ?api-version=1.0 is easy to add but clutters URLs. Header versioning with api-version: 1.0 keeps URLs clean but is harder to test. Media-type versioning via the Accept header is the most REST-pure but the least discoverable.
Which would you choose for a new public API and why?

URL path versioning. Public APIs need discoverability - developers test them in browsers and curl, not custom tooling. The version in the URL is visible in documentation, shareable in links, and cache-friendly because the URL itself changes per version.
How do you sunset a version without breaking clients overnight?

I mark it with [ApiVersion('1.0', Deprecated = true)] in the Asp.Versioning package. The framework adds a deprecation warning header to every response so client developers see it in their tooling. I publish a sunset date, monitor usage metrics to confirm migration is done, then remove the version.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is idempotency and which HTTP methods are idempotent?

answer.oneLiner: An idempotent operation produces the same end state no matter how many times you call it. GET, PUT, DELETE, and HEAD are idempotent. POST is not. This matters because it tells you which operations are safe to retry.
answer.detail: Idempotency is about the end state, not the response. DELETE /orders/42 the first time deletes the order and returns 200 or 204. DELETE it again and the order is already gone - you might get 404, but the end state is the same: no order 42 exists. That makes DELETE idempotent. GET and HEAD read data without side effects. PUT replaces the resource with the body you send - the same body repeatedly gives the same end state. POST typically creates a new resource each call - POST /orders three times creates three orders. This is why retry logic matters in distributed systems. A network timeout on a PUT or DELETE is safe to retry. A timeout on a POST could mean the server processed it successfully but the response got lost - retrying would create a duplicate order. The solution for POST is idempotency keys: the client generates a unique key (UUID), sends it as a header like Idempotency-Key, and the server stores the result. A repeated request with the same key returns the stored result without re-processing.
keyPoints: Idempotent means calling an operation multiple times has the same end state as calling it once
GET, PUT, DELETE, and HEAD are idempotent - safe to retry on network failure
POST is not idempotent - retrying a timed-out POST can create duplicates in e-commerce or payments
Use client-supplied idempotency keys to make POST safe to retry in critical flows

code.note: The idempotency key is checked before processing. A duplicate request returns the original result without re-charging.

answer.analogy: Pressing the elevator button ten times does not make it arrive faster - the end state is the same.
redFlags: Saying POST is idempotent - it is not; each call typically creates a new resource
Confusing idempotent with safe - GET is both idempotent and safe (no side effects); DELETE is idempotent but not safe (it deletes)
Not knowing idempotency keys as a pattern for making POST safe in payment or order flows
Saying DELETE always returns 200 on repeat calls - it may return 404 on the second call, but the end state is still idempotent

followUpsQA.q: Why does idempotency matter for retry logic?
followUpsQA.a: Networks are unreliable. If a client retries a timed-out request, idempotent operations (GET, PUT, DELETE) are safe because the end state does not change. Retrying a non-idempotent POST can create duplicate records or double charges.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between safe and idempotent?
followUpsQA.a: Safe means no side effects at all - GET and HEAD read without changing state. Idempotent means repeating produces the same end state - DELETE is idempotent but not safe because it does change state the first time.

followUpsQA.q: How do you implement an idempotency key?
followUpsQA.a: The client generates a UUID and sends it as an Idempotency-Key header. The server checks a cache or database for that key before processing. If found, it returns the stored result. If not, it processes and stores the result against that key.

followUpsQA.q: Is PATCH idempotent?
followUpsQA.a: Not inherently. A PATCH that sets a field to a fixed value is idempotent. A PATCH that increments a counter is not, because each call changes the result.

dialogue.text: What is idempotency and which HTTP methods are idempotent?
An idempotent operation produces the same end state no matter how many times you call it. GET, PUT, DELETE, and HEAD are idempotent. POST is not - each call typically creates a new resource.
Why does this matter in practice?

It determines whether it is safe to retry. If a network timeout hits during a PUT, I can safely retry - the end state is the same. If a timeout hits during a POST /payments, retrying risks charging the customer twice. That is why payment systems like Stripe use idempotency keys.
How do idempotency keys work?

The client generates a UUID and sends it as an Idempotency-Key header. Before processing, the server checks a cache or database for that key. If it exists, it returns the stored result - no double-processing. If not, it processes and stores the result against that key with a 24-hour TTL.
What is the difference between safe and idempotent?

Safe means no side effects at all - GET reads data without changing anything. Idempotent means repeating gives the same end state, but the operation may still have a side effect the first time. DELETE is idempotent - the order is gone whether you call it once or ten times - but it is not safe because it does change state.
DELETE /orders/42 returns 404 on the second call. Is it still idempotent?

Yes. Idempotency is about the end state, not the response code. After both calls, no order 42 exists - the end state is identical. The 404 on the second call is technically acceptable, though some APIs return 204 consistently to make client retry logic simpler.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How does model binding and validation work in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: Model binding automatically maps incoming request data from the route, query string, body, form, or headers onto your action method parameters. With the [ApiController] attribute, validation from data annotations runs automatically and returns a 400 Bad Request if anything fails.
answer.detail: Model binding is the process of extracting data from an HTTP request and converting it into .NET types. ASP.NET Core reads from multiple sources: [FromRoute] for URL segments like {customerId}, [FromQuery] for query string parameters, [FromBody] for the JSON request body, [FromForm] for multipart form data, and [FromHeader] for HTTP headers. With [ApiController], the framework infers binding sources - complex types default to [FromBody] so you rarely need to annotate them. Validation runs after binding. Data annotations on the DTO or model class - [Required], [Range], [StringLength], [EmailAddress] - are evaluated against the bound model. If any fail, [ApiController] automatically short-circuits the action and returns a 400 Bad Request with a ValidationProblemDetails body listing all errors. Without [ApiController] you must check ModelState.IsValid manually in every action. The important security point is to always bind to a DTO, never directly to your EF Core entity. Binding directly to an entity enables over-posting attacks where a client sets fields like IsAdmin or CreatedDate that they should not control.
keyPoints: Binding sources: [FromRoute], [FromQuery], [FromBody], [FromForm], [FromHeader] - [ApiController] infers most automatically
[ApiController] auto-returns 400 with ValidationProblemDetails when model state is invalid - no manual check needed
Data annotations like [Required], [Range], and [StringLength] define validation rules on the DTO
Always bind to a DTO, never to an EF entity - binding to entities enables over-posting attacks

code.note: The DTO exposes only the fields the client is allowed to set, and [ApiController] validates them before the action body runs.

answer.analogy: Model binding is the waiter who takes your order; validation is the kitchen that refuses impossible requests.
redFlags: Saying you check ModelState.IsValid in every action - [ApiController] makes that unnecessary and its absence is a red flag
Binding directly to EF entities - this enables over-posting where clients set fields like IsAdmin they should never control
Not knowing the five binding source attributes: [FromRoute], [FromQuery], [FromBody], [FromForm], [FromHeader]
Confusing model binding with model validation - binding extracts and converts data; validation then checks the rules

followUpsQA.q: What does [ApiController] do when ModelState is invalid?
followUpsQA.a: It automatically returns a 400 Bad Request with a ValidationProblemDetails body listing all validation errors, before the action method body ever runs. You do not need to check ModelState.IsValid manually.

followUpsQA.q: What is an over-posting attack and how does binding to a DTO prevent it?
followUpsQA.a: An over-posting attack is when a client sends extra fields not intended to be updatable - for example, setting IsAdmin=true on a user object. Binding to a DTO that only exposes allowed fields means extra fields in the request body are simply ignored.

followUpsQA.q: How does [ApiController] infer the binding source for complex types?
followUpsQA.a: Complex types default to [FromBody], so the framework reads the JSON request body automatically. Simple types in action parameters default to [FromRoute] or [FromQuery] depending on whether they match a route template segment.

followUpsQA.q: How do you add custom validation logic beyond data annotations?
followUpsQA.a: Implement IValidatableObject on the DTO class, or create a custom ValidationAttribute. For complex cross-field rules, many teams use FluentValidation which gives a richer API and better separation of concerns.

dialogue.text: How does model binding and validation work in ASP.NET Core?

Model binding maps request data from the route, query string, JSON body, form, or headers onto action parameters. With [ApiController], validation from data annotations runs automatically after binding. If anything is invalid, it returns a 400 with a ValidationProblemDetails body before the action runs.
How does the framework know where to pull each value from?

From binding source attributes: [FromRoute], [FromQuery], [FromBody], [FromForm], [FromHeader]. With [ApiController], the framework infers most of them - complex types default to [FromBody] and simple types match route segments or query string automatically. You only annotate when you need to override.
You mentioned binding to a DTO. What happens if you bind directly to your EF entity?

You open yourself to over-posting attacks. A client can send fields like IsAdmin, CreatedBy, or IsDeleted in the JSON body and the model binder will populate them onto the entity. Binding to a DTO that only exposes the fields the client is allowed to set means extra fields in the request are silently ignored.
What if you forget the [ApiController] attribute?

Automatic 400-on-invalid-ModelState stops working. You would have to manually check if (!ModelState.IsValid) return BadRequest(ModelState) in every action, which is easy to forget. You also lose automatic binding source inference and automatic multipart/form-data binding.
How would you add a custom validation rule that checks two fields together?

I would either implement IValidatableObject on the DTO class - which runs after all annotation checks and gives access to all fields - or use FluentValidation. FluentValidation is my preference for complex rules because it keeps validation logic out of the model class and is easier to test in isolation.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅
